text: Hello, this is Dr. Intavi. Today I will be discussing a laparoscopic repair of perforated peptic ulcer.
This patient presented with sepsis and free air on the CT scan.
The surgeon performs a laparoscopic repair of a perforated peptic ulcer using three small incisions: two 5mm ports and one 10mm port.
Upon entering the abdominal cavity, the surgeon encounters a significant amount of pus and bile, which is being cleared from the surgical field.

The video shows widespread inflammation and erythema throughout the abdominal cavity, including the abdominal wall, gallbladder, and liver. Fibrinous material is visible, suggesting the perforation occurred hours earlier.
The surgeon removes the omentum covering the perforated peptic ulcer, revealing the ulcer site. The surgeon prepares to suture the ulcer using an indoor stitch technique.
The surgeon visually assesses a small peptic ulcer, approximately two millimeters in width, and attempts to close it primarily using sutures.
The surgeon places full-thickness sutures to close the small peptic ulcer, ensuring adequate grip for closure.
The surgeon places the omentum over the peptic ulcer site to buttress the closure, providing additional support to the primary suture repair.
